title: Tensor.dtype
text: Every torch.Tensor has a dtype attribute that indicates its data type. Access it with tensor.dtype, which returns a torch.dtype object such as torch.float32, torch.float64, torch.int64, torch.bool, and others. To check whether a tensor has a specific dtype, compare directly: tensor.dtype == torch.float32. The dtype determines the precision and range of values the tensor can hold. torch.float32 is the default dtype for floating-point tensors created by most factory functions like torch.zeros, torch.ones, and torch.randn. torch.float64 provides higher precision but uses twice the memory. torch.float16 and torch.bfloat16 are commonly used for mixed-precision training to reduce memory and increase throughput on supported hardware. Integer types include torch.int64, torch.int32, torch.int16, and torch.int8. To change a tensor's dtype, use tensor.to(dtype=torch.float16) or tensor.float(), tensor.half(), tensor.int(), and similar convenience methods. Note that changing dtype always produces a new tensor with copied data; it is not an in-place operation on the storage. When performing operations between tensors, PyTorch requires compatible dtypes in many cases. For example, torch.matmul requires both inputs to have the same dtype. If they differ, you must explicitly cast one tensor before the operation.

title: Tensor.to
text: Tensor.to(*args, **kwargs) performs dtype and device conversion on a tensor. It can be called in several forms: tensor.to(dtype), tensor.to(device), tensor.to(device, dtype), or tensor.to(other_tensor) to match another tensor's dtype and device. For example, tensor.to(torch.float16) changes the dtype, tensor.to('cuda') moves to GPU, and tensor.to('cuda', torch.float16) does both in a single call. If the tensor already has the requested dtype and is already on the requested device, .to() returns the same tensor without copying. Otherwise, a new tensor is created with the converted data. PyTorch does not automatically move tensors between devices. If one tensor is on cpu and another is on cuda, any operation between them will fail with a device mismatch error. You must explicitly move tensors to the same device before operating on them. The typical pattern is tensor_b = tensor_b.to(tensor_a.device) or tensor = tensor.to('cuda'). Similarly, PyTorch does not automatically cast dtypes for most operations. If you attempt torch.matmul on a float32 tensor and a float16 tensor, it will raise a RuntimeError about dtype mismatch. You must cast one operand first: a.to(b.dtype) or b.to(a.dtype). The .to() method is the unified way to handle both device and dtype conversion.

title: torch.float32
text: torch.float32 is the default floating-point dtype in PyTorch, also accessible as torch.float. It uses 32 bits per element and provides a good balance between precision and memory. Most tensor factory functions such as torch.zeros, torch.ones, torch.randn, and torch.tensor with float inputs produce torch.float32 tensors by default. To create a tensor with a different dtype, pass the dtype argument explicitly: torch.zeros(3, dtype=torch.float16). To check if a tensor uses float32, compare tensor.dtype == torch.float32. torch.float16, also known as half precision, uses 16 bits and is widely used in mixed-precision training with torch.cuda.amp. torch.bfloat16 is another 16-bit format with a larger exponent range, supported on newer GPUs and TPUs. torch.float64, also known as double precision, uses 64 bits and is used when higher numerical precision is required, such as in scientific computing. When mixing dtypes in operations, PyTorch generally does not auto-promote. For example, adding a torch.float32 tensor to a torch.float64 tensor may work with promotion, but torch.matmul between torch.float32 and torch.float16 will raise an error. Always verify dtype compatibility before performing operations.

title: torch.float16
text: torch.float16 is a 16-bit floating-point dtype, also accessible as torch.half. It uses half the memory of torch.float32 and is significantly faster on GPUs that support native half-precision arithmetic, such as NVIDIA GPUs with Tensor Cores. torch.float16 is the primary dtype used in mixed-precision training workflows, typically via torch.cuda.amp.autocast, which automatically runs eligible operations in float16 while keeping others in float32 for numerical stability. To convert a tensor to float16, use tensor.to(torch.float16) or tensor.half(). To move a tensor to cuda and convert to float16 in one call, use tensor.to('cuda', torch.float16) or equivalently tensor.to(device='cuda', dtype=torch.float16). The reduced precision of float16 means it has a smaller representable range and lower decimal accuracy compared to float32. Values outside the representable range will overflow to infinity or underflow to zero. For this reason, loss scaling is often used alongside float16 training to prevent gradient underflow. When performing operations, both operands generally must have the same dtype. torch.matmul between a float32 tensor and a float16 tensor will raise a dtype mismatch error. Cast one operand first using .to(dtype) before the operation.

title: torch.matmul
text: torch.matmul(input, other) performs matrix multiplication between two tensors. The behavior depends on the dimensionality of the inputs: for two 1D tensors it computes the dot product, for two 2D tensors it computes the standard matrix product, and for higher-dimensional tensors it computes batched matrix products with broadcasting. Both input tensors must have the same dtype. If one tensor is torch.float32 and the other is torch.float16, torch.matmul will raise a RuntimeError indicating that the dtypes do not match. PyTorch does not automatically cast operands to a common dtype for matmul. You must explicitly convert one operand before calling matmul: either a = a.to(b.dtype) or b = b.to(a.dtype). Both tensors must also be on the same device. If one is on cpu and the other is on cuda, matmul will fail with a device mismatch error. Move tensors to the same device first using .to(device). For 2D inputs with shapes [m, k] and [k, n], the result has shape [m, n]. For batched inputs, the batch dimensions are broadcast following standard broadcasting rules, and the last two dimensions follow matrix multiplication rules: [..., m, k] multiplied by [..., k, n] produces [..., m, n]. torch.mm is a strict 2D-only variant that does not support broadcasting. Use torch.matmul for general-purpose matrix multiplication.
